html:
Taki Chemical (4025 JP, ¥5,450)

Taki is no longer the hidden sub-book stock it was before the May 8 Q1, but the thesis is still alive because the market is valuing only the earnings bounce, not the transition underneath it. At the May 15 close of ¥5,450, the stock is up sharply from the pre-result ¥4,605 level, yet it still trades around 1.0x Q1 book value and at a modest multiple of current-cycle earnings if management’s FY2026 guide proves conservative.

The key correction to the prior thesis: this is not a clearly disclosed crystalline-cellulose or croscarmellose pharma-excipient compounder. The real business is more prosaic, but also more durable: water treatment chemicals, fertilizer, functional materials, real estate, and logistics. The life-science angle exists, but it is option value, not today’s core profit engine. Q1 specifically mentions new finished goods for pharmaceutical additives inside Functional Materials, and Taki also develops medical-grade biodegradable polymers and collagen materials, but the current profit engine remains chemicals and water treatment.

The variant perception is that investors still screen Taki as a small Japanese chemical cyclical with a one-off earnings rebound. The better view is that Taki is a water-infrastructure and environmental-chemicals company with a cleaner balance sheet, a live capital-efficiency plan, and an underappreciated capacity cycle in ultra-high-basicity PAC. Taki says it developed polyaluminum chloride in 1962 and has long held a top-maker position, with PAC used in drinking water, sewage, industrial water, wastewater, sludge filtration, and process coagulation. This is not glamorous, but it is recurring, regulated, and hard to displace once embedded in municipal and industrial procurement.

The May 8 Q1 changed the setup. Revenue rose 9.2%, operating profit rose 37.1%, and ordinary profit rose 27.4% to ¥983 million. The Chemicals segment was the standout: sales rose 12.6% and segment profit rose 51.5% to ¥589 million, driven by water-treatment price adjustments and new pharmaceutical-additive finished goods in Functional Materials. Management left full-year guidance unchanged at ¥43.0 billion sales, ¥2.45 billion operating profit, ¥3.05 billion ordinary profit, and ¥317.79 EPS. That means Q1 already delivered 38.6% of guided operating profit and 32.2% of guided ordinary profit.

The stock reaction was violent because the market had been anchored to the February guide, which called for a 22.6% operating-profit decline despite FY2025 being a record year. The guide may still include capacity-expansion costs and a conservative view of raw-material and pricing conditions, but the first quarter says the business is absorbing those costs better than expected. The May 15 close of ¥5,450 is still only 5.3% above the May 8 close of ¥5,180, so the market has repriced the surprise but has not fully capitalized a higher earnings base.

The transition matters. Taki’s 2025 results were already strong: sales of ¥41.98 billion, operating profit of ¥3.16 billion, ordinary profit of ¥3.78 billion, net income of ¥3.28 billion, EPS of ¥389, and ROE of 8.1%. Chemicals generated ¥2.31 billion of segment profit, Real Estate generated ¥721 million, Agri generated ¥485 million, and Transportation generated ¥308 million. This is a messier business than a pure specialty-chemical peer, but the messy structure hides stable profit pools. Real estate alone is a high-margin shopping-center rental asset. Chemicals are a mix of water-treatment infrastructure, high-purity tantalum and niobium oxides, aluminum compounds, and nanomaterials. Agri is cyclical, but its FY2025 rebound showed operating leverage.

The February medium-term plan revision is the real tell. Taki raised its 2028 targets to ¥44 billion sales, ¥3.5 billion operating profit, and ROE of at least 7.0%. That looks intentionally beatable: FY2025 sales were already ¥42.0 billion, operating profit was ¥3.16 billion, and ROE was 8.1%. The plan specifically calls out water-treatment capacity expansion, ultra-high-basicity PAC market penetration, environmental coagulant development, life-science expansion, M&A, and overseas market development. This is not a heroic growth story. It is a modest-capacity, price-realization, and capital-efficiency story where small improvements move the equity value because the float-adjusted market cap is only about ¥45 billion.

The balance sheet is the second pillar. At March 31, 2026, Taki had ¥44.9 billion of owners’ equity, or roughly ¥5,390 per share excluding treasury shares. Cash and deposits were ¥6.8 billion, investment securities were ¥20.2 billion, land was carried at ¥7.7 billion, and the equity ratio was 65.7%. Debt is small. The current stock price is essentially book value, but book includes a large listed-securities portfolio and old land. That is not the same as a leveraged chemical stock on a peak multiple.

Capital allocation is no longer theoretical. In its February capital-cost and stock-price response, management explicitly acknowledged that PBR had been below 1.0x since September 2024 and blamed insufficient communication of growth strategy. It committed to progressive dividends, a 30% or higher consolidated payout-ratio target, flexible buybacks, improving total shareholder return, and reducing strategic shareholdings to below 20% of consolidated net assets by FY2030, with sale proceeds prioritized for growth investment and shareholder returns. FY2025 already included about ¥0.7 billion of treasury-share purchases, and the FY2026 dividend guide is ¥80.

The opportunity exists because the market’s tools are still crude. After the Q1 spike, data portals can call the stock “expensive” on the unchanged company EPS guide, while retail chatter focuses on the short-term 12.5% May 8 jump and “stop-high” momentum. That misses the better question: if Q1 strength is not a seasonal fluke, what is Taki worth when normalized operating profit is ¥3.5-4.0 billion, the real estate and securities portfolio are treated as monetizable assets, and buybacks start to shrink the share count?

Downside is not imaginary. The stock has already rerated from ¥3,550 in March to ¥5,450. If raw materials spike, if water-treatment price pass-through lags, or if management’s FY2026 caution is accurate, the stock can fall back toward 0.8x book. Liquidity is thin, and the market may not reward strategic-share reductions until cash is visibly returned. The life-science materials angle could also remain too small to matter for years.

But the forward risk/reward still works because the bear case is asset-backed while the upside case is earnings-backed. A company with recurring water-treatment demand, rising PAC capacity, record FY2025 earnings, Q1 operating profit up 37%, ¥20 billion of investment securities, net cash, a real estate profit stream, and an explicit TSE-style capital-efficiency plan should not be capped at book value if ROE can stay near or above 8%.

My targets use the May 15, 2026 close of ¥5,450 as the current price.

Bear case: ¥4,300, down 21%. FY2026 guidance is right, Q1 was seasonally front-loaded, margins compress, and the stock falls back to about 0.8x current book.

Base case: ¥6,700, up 23%. Operating profit lands closer to ¥3.4-3.7 billion, management raises guidance later in 2026, book value grows, and the market pays roughly 1.2x forward book or 15x normalized EPS around ¥430-450.

Bull case: ¥8,500, up 56%. Ultra-high-basicity PAC capacity tightens the water-treatment franchise, Chemicals sustains double-digit margins, strategic-share sales fund a meaningful buyback, life-science and functional materials gain credibility, and the market rerates Taki to 1.4-1.5x book or mid-teens earnings on ¥525-560 EPS.
